claim: Claude Opus 4.5 is specifically trained to be a better prompt engineer for sub-agents—it can write better prompts to delegate work to other agents
actionable: Use Claude Opus 4.5 as your orchestrator agent when building multi-agent systems because it excels at writing prompts for and delegating to sub-agents.

claim: Deploy multiple Opus 4.5 sub-agents simultaneously with browser automation for parallel testing and research—each agent operates its own browser instance
actionable: Use Claude Code to spawn multiple Opus 4.5 sub-agents running browser automation in parallel for distributed testing and verification tasks.

claim: Anthropic cut Opus pricing by one-third while maintaining state-of-the-art performance, making it cost-effective for production agentic systems
actionable: Choose Claude Opus 4.5 for production deployments because it offers 60 tokens per second throughput at reduced pricing compared to previous versions.

claim: Better planning (expressed as step-by-step execution plans) is more important than raw autonomy hours—agents should show plans before executing long tasks
actionable: Design agentic workflows to show explicit plans to users before executing, prioritizing plan quality over uninterrupted autonomy duration.

claim: You can one-shot full-stack applications (plan, build, host, test) with Opus 4.5 in a single prompt using agent sandboxes
actionable: Write comprehensive prompts that instruct Opus 4.5 to plan, build, host, and test full-stack applications end-to-end in one execution.

claim: Use agent sandboxes (isolated environments like E2B) to give agents their own dedicated computing resources for better isolation, scale, and autonomy
actionable: Deploy your agents within isolated sandbox environments to give them dedicated resources and prevent cross-agent interference.

claim: Build browser UI testing at scale by spinning up multiple agents to test user stories in parallel—this increases confidence in agent-built code
actionable: Create test workflows where multiple agents run different user story tests in parallel, then report failures back to the main agent for debugging.

claim: Premium pricing for premium compute is justified—faster, more intelligent models save engineering time and complete tasks impossible for cheaper models
actionable: Choose Opus 4.5 over cheaper models when the task complexity justifies the cost, measuring savings in engineering time and capability gains.

claim: The agent is the new compositional unit of engineering—mastering agents replaces mastering prompts as the core skill for knowledge workers
actionable: Shift your focus from prompt engineering to agent orchestration: learn to build, delegate to, and coordinate multiple agents for complex work.

claim: Agentic workflow progression: learn to operate single agents, then operate better agents (via prompt engineering), then operate more agents (via delegation and orchestration)
actionable: Progress your agent skills in stages: first master single-agent prompting, then upgrade to better models, then scale via multi-agent systems and orchestration.
